components.label: Web/API Client
components.description: Sends prompt via HTTPS POST; receives streaming token response via SSE (Server-Sent Events); handles reconnection on stream drop

components.label: API Gateway (Rate Limiter + Auth)
components.description: Authenticates API keys, enforces token-bucket rate limits (60 req/min free, 3600 req/min paid) using Redis INCR+EXPIRE, routes requests to inference workers

components.label: Inference Worker (GPU Server)
components.description: Runs LLM inference; holds KV-cache (attention state) in GPU memory during streaming; streams tokens back via SSE; stateful for the duration of one request

components.label: Session Cache + Semantic Cache (Redis + Vector DB)
components.description: Redis stores conversation history per session_id for multi-turn context; Vector DB (Pinecone/pgvector) stores prompt embeddings for semantic cache lookups (similarity > 0.95 returns cached response)

components.label: User DB + Billing DB
components.description: User DB stores API key metadata, tier, and usage quotas; Billing DB stores aggregated token counts per API key per billing period

connections.label: Check semantic cache + rate limit bucket in Redis
Route to available GPU inference worker (sticky)
Read/write conversation history for multi-turn context
Write token usage event to billing queue

keyInsights: GPU KV-cache stickiness: the attention key-value state built up during token generation lives in GPU VRAM; sticky routing ensures the same GPU handles all tokens in a request — routing to a different GPU would require serialising and transferring gigabytes of GPU state

SSE over WebSockets for streaming: SSE is unidirectional (server to client), works over standard HTTP/2, multiplexes over a single connection, and auto-reconnects with Last-Event-ID header — perfect for one-way token streaming with no bidirectional signalling needed

Semantic cache with vector similarity: embedding the prompt into a high-dimensional vector and storing it in a vector DB allows returning cached responses for prompts that are semantically equivalent even if not lexically identical (e.g. 'What is the capital of France?' and 'France capital city?')

Token bucket rate limiting atomicity: Redis INCR increments the request counter and returns the new value in one operation; combined with EXPIRE in a Lua script, this is atomic — no two concurrent requests from the same API key can both pass a limit that only allows one more

Async billing decoupling: writing token counts synchronously in the inference hot path would add 10-50ms of DB latency to every request; instead a Kafka event carries (api_key, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens) and is aggregated by a downstream billing service every minute

tradeoffs.decision: Sticky GPU routing over stateless routing
tradeoffs.pro: KV-cache reuse across multi-turn conversations dramatically reduces GPU compute; no state serialisation overhead
tradeoffs.con: Uneven GPU utilisation if some conversations are much longer than others; requires consistent hashing in the load balancer and graceful drain on GPU node shutdown

tradeoffs.decision: SSE over WebSockets for token streaming
tradeoffs.pro: Works over standard HTTP/2, no special proxying needed; auto-reconnect built into browser EventSource API; unidirectional simplicity
tradeoffs.con: Cannot receive client messages mid-stream (e.g. 'stop generating'); follow-up messages require a new HTTP request

tradeoffs.pro: Catches paraphrase variants of popular prompts; significantly higher cache hit rate than exact key matching
tradeoffs.con: Embedding model adds 5-20ms per request; vector similarity search adds latency; false positives can return semantically close but contextually wrong cached answers

antiPatterns: Writing billing records synchronously in the inference request handler — adds DB latency to the hot path and risks dropping tokens if the billing write fails

Using WebSocket for token streaming when SSE is sufficient — WebSockets require stateful connection management and bidirectional protocol handling; SSE is simpler and works over standard HTTP infrastructure
Routing all requests to a pool of stateless servers without sticky routing — each token generation must start from scratch without KV-cache; GPU utilisation drops and latency doubles

Storing conversation history in the relational DB and reading it on every token — at 500 tokens per response and 50 tokens/sec, this generates 10 DB reads per second per active conversation; conversation history belongs in Redis with a TTL
